summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is a wave?

A wave is a disturbance that transfers energy through a medium (or space) without transferring matter. The other options — 'A movement of matter', 'A type of force', and 'A form of temperature' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

What is a transverse wave?

Transverse waves — Oscillation perpendicular to wave direction (light, water surface waves). Longitudinal waves — Oscillation parallel to wave direction (sound, compression springs). The other options — 'Oscillation parallel to wave direction', 'A wave that doesn't move', and 'A wave in a circle' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

What is wavelength (λ)?

Wave properties: v = fλ (velocity = frequency × wavelength). Amplitude determines energy/intensity. Frequency and wavelength are inversely related at constant wave speed. The answer is 'Distance between consecutive identical points'. The other options — 'The height of a wave', 'The speed of a wave', and 'The time for one cycle' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: Give an example of a longitudinal wave.

Longitudinal waves — Oscillation parallel to wave direction (sound, compression springs). The other options — 'Light', 'Water surface waves', and 'Radio waves' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Special Topics
summary_sections.content_html:
Important Concepts

If frequency doubles but speed stays the same, wavelength:

Wave properties: v = fλ (velocity = frequency × wavelength). Amplitude determines energy/intensity. Frequency and wavelength are inversely related at constant wave speed. The answer is 'Halves'. The other options — 'Doubles', 'Stays the same', and 'Quadruples' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.
